@graph.name: BMW X5 xDrive40i Engine Era Map

mainEntity.name: How much does a BMW X5 xDrive40i engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned B58 engine typically costs £4,500–£8,500 supply only depending on the year, with the 2024+ LCI version sitting at £5,500–£8,500. Fitting adds £1,100–£1,600. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your car.

mainEntity.name: Is the BMW X5 xDrive40i engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The B58 is rated ★★★★☆ — it's widely regarded as one of BMW's most reliable modern six-cylinders. Unlike the N55 it replaced, the B58 has no major inherited failure modes. However, known issues include water pump and coolant system leaks, carbon buildup, wastegate actuator corrosion (pre-June 2024), and oil leaks. Regular maintenance is essential.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the BMW X5 xDrive40i?

acceptedAnswer.text: Water pump and coolant system leaks, intake valve carbon buildup requiring walnut blasting, wastegate actuator corrosion causing rattle (pre-June 2024), valve cover and oil filter housing oil leaks, and PCV system issues. None typically lead to full engine replacement if caught early.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a BMW X5 xDrive40i engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Against a well-kept G05 X5 xDrive40i worth £18,000–£35,000, a £5,500 recon is a manageable 16–30% of the vehicle's value — worth it for a well-maintained example. Given the X5's strong retained values, replacement is usually viable for vehicles in good condition.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my BMW X5 xDrive40i engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation. The xDrive40i uses the B58B30C 3.0-litre turbocharged inline-six petrol engine. Tell us your registration date and we'll confirm it for free.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair the B58's water pump, or does it need a new engine?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes — the water pump is a straightforward repair costing £500–£1,200. The B58's water pump is the most common service item on this engine; replacing it as a preventative measure at 60,000 miles is strongly recommended. If you notice low coolant or a leak, book an inspection immediately to prevent overheating damage.

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: BMW X5 xDrive40i — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: If caught early; regular maintenance prevents severe buildup
Turbo replacement; warranty extension may apply to some vehicles
Straightforward gasket replacement; catch it early
Preventative — before oil damages the serpentine belt
Integrated into valve cover; replace as a unit
Not a common B58 failure — if it occurs, replacement is the only option

buyingChecks.title: Buying This X5 xDrive40i? Check These First
buyIf: * Full BMW service history with documented oil-change intervals
* 2024+ LCI for the 375 bhp output and updated components
* Evidence of regular maintenance — the B58 rewards care
* Post-June 2024 build to avoid the wastegate actuator corrosion issue
avoidIf: * Service history shows repeated coolant top-ups with no diagnosis (water pump risk)
* Metallic rattle at idle or low RPM (wastegate actuator issue)
* Oil leaks with no record of repair
* Engine warning light present with no diagnosis documentation

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The X5 xDrive40i's B58 engine is widely regarded as one of BMW's most reliable modern six-cylinders — a significant step up from the N55 it replaced. However, like any engine, it has known maintenance items: water pump and coolant system leaks, carbon buildup, wastegate actuator corrosion (pre-June 2024), and oil leaks. Buy on service history and proof of preventative maintenance. If you're replacing, a reconditioned unit with a 12-month warranty is the sweet spot.

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used BMW X5 xDrive40i (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Cold Start — Listen for any rattle on startup (wastegate actuator or timing chain — B58 chain issues are rare)
* Service History — Including which engine era (B58 is the only xDrive40i engine — verify this before purchase)
* Coolant Level — Is the coolant low with no visible leak? Water pump or coolant system risk
* Oil Leaks — Check for oil spots under the car or a burning oil smell (valve cover or oil filter housing gaskets)
* Idle Rattle — Any metallic rattle at 800–1,500 RPM? Wastegate actuator corrosion (pre-June 2024)
* Throttle Response — Any hesitation under light throttle? Carbon buildup indicator
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? Any unresolved cooling, fuelling, or emissions issues?
* MOT History — Check for emissions-related failures; often point to carbon buildup or turbo issues
